title: You've already used AI today.
body: Every time you Google something, get a Netflix recommendation, or ask ChatGPT a question, AI is running behind the scenes. But most people have no idea what it's actually doing. So... what IS it doing?
prompt: How often do you use AI tools in a typical day?

title: What is a Language Model?
body:
AI isn't thinking. It's predicting. When you type a message, it doesn't 'understand' you the way a human would. It's doing one thing: predicting what word should come next, over and over again.

You type: 'The weather today is...'
AI predicts: 'sunny' / 'cold' / 'unpredictable'
prompt: If you typed 'Peanut butter and ___', what word do you think AI would predict?

title: Training Data
body:
How does AI know anything? Before you ever talked to it, the AI read. A lot. Billions of web pages, books, articles, Wikipedia, Reddit, news sites - essentially a huge chunk of the internet.

Think of it like a kid who grew up reading every book in every library in the world.
prompt: Which source do you think teaches AI the most?

title: Tokens & Patterns
body: AI doesn't read words. It reads chunks. It breaks text into pieces called 'tokens' - roughly parts of words - and learned patterns between billions of them. That's how it knows 'peanut butter and ___' is almost always 'jelly.'
prompt: Why chop words into smaller pieces?
choices: To share patterns between related words

title: Estimation
body: AI doesn't know things. It estimates them. When you ask a question, it doesn't look up an answer - it generates the most statistically likely response. That's why it can sound confident while being completely wrong.
prompt: When AI sounds very confident, what should you do?
